title: Banoffee cheesecake
subtitle: No-bake, biscuit base with dulce de leche and banana
excerpt: A fridge-set cheesecake with a digestive biscuit base, a cream cheese and double cream filling with dulce de leche folded through, and banana and cream on top. No gelatine, no oven.
sourceNotes: Banoffee pie was created at The Hungry Monk restaurant in Sussex in 1972. The cheesecake variant is a domestic adaptation: the pastry base becomes a biscuit base, the condensed-milk toffee stays, and the cream filling incorporates cream cheese for stability. This version follows the standard British domestic formula for no-bake cheesecake.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make the night before and refrigerate overnight. Add banana and cream topping up to 2 hours before serving.

glossaryTerms.definition: A thick, amber-coloured spread made from slowly heating sweetened condensed milk until the sugars caramelise. It has the texture of thick jam and a distinctive toffee flavour. Sold ready-made in tins and squeezable pouches at most UK supermarkets.

content.text: No-bake cheesecake works on one principle: whipped double cream folded into cream cheese gives the filling enough structure to hold its shape when refrigerated, without gelatine. The double cream must be whipped to soft peaks, not stiff — over-whipped cream folds in grainily and the cheesecake sets with a slightly grainy texture.

goes into the filling as a swirl rather than a thorough mix, which gives a ripple effect when the cheesecake is cut. Add the banana to the top no more than 2 hours before serving; it will brown if it sits longer.

Crush the {{digestive-biscuits}} to fine crumbs — in a food processor or in a sealed bag with a rolling pin. Mix with the melted {{unsalted-butter}} until the crumbs are evenly dampened. Press into the base of the springform tin in an even layer. Refrigerate while you make the filling.

Beat the {{full-fat-cream-cheese}} and {{icing-sugar}} together until smooth. In a separate bowl, whip the 300 ml of {{double-cream}} with the {{vanilla-extract}} to soft peaks: the cream should hold its shape when you lift the beaters but the tips should curl over rather than stand upright. Fold the whipped cream into the cream cheese in three additions.

Add the 200 g of {{dulce-de-leche}} and fold it through the filling with just three or four strokes — enough to create a ripple, not to blend it evenly. Spoon onto the biscuit base and smooth the top. Refrigerate for at least 6 hours, overnight if possible.

Run a warm knife around the inside of the tin before unclipping. Arrange the sliced {{bananas}} over the top, spoon or pipe the lightly whipped cream over the banana, and drizzle over the extra {{dulce-de-leche}} if using. Serve immediately or within 2 hours.
